coredata.dc:title: Functionalized carbon dots with guanidine salt ionic liquid regulate oxidative damage and amyloid aggregation

Hou, Tongtong
Shao, Xu
Wang, Chaoli
Wang, Xin
Guan, Ping
Wu, Yaoguo
Hu, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: An imbalance in the brain microenvironment, involving oxidative stress and β-amyloid (Aβ) accumulation, is thought to be one of the primary characteristics of early Alzheimer's disease (AD). To address the intricate pathophysiology of AD, therapeutic approaches that can concurrently control several diseases in the AD microenvironment are desperately needed. This study created a guanidine salt ionic liquid functionalized carbon dots (CDs@TGM-IL) to mitigate Aβ aggregation-induced cytotoxicity and scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) simultaneously. In vitro studies have shown that CDs@TGM-IL can effectively inhibit Aβ42 protein aggregation, disaggregate mature Aβ42 fibrils, and effectively remove ROS. In vivo studies have found that CDs@TGM-IL can cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and improve cognitive performance in AD mice. Just as importantly, CDs@TGM-IL has been shown to have unparalleled biocompatibility. This means that CDs@TGM-IL is expected to be a possible treatment for AD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquid
Carbon dots
Alzheimer's disease
Oxidative stress

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inhibition of Aβ42 and lysozyme protein aggregation
Disaggregation of mature Aβ42 fibrils and lysozyme fibrils
The scavenging performance of ROS by CDs@TGM-IL
Cross the BBB and removal of amyloid plaques in the brain

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and structural characterization of CDs@TGM-IL. (a)TEM image of CDs (inset: size distribution histogram). (b)TEM image of CDs@TGM-IL (inset: size distribution histogram). (c) AFM image of CDs (inset: height distribution). (d) AFM image of CDs@TGM-IL(inset: height distribution). (e-h) XPS full spectrum, and high-resolution C1s, high-resolution O1s and high-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs@TGM-IL. (i) FT-IR spectrum of CDs and CDs@TGM-IL. (j) Raman spectrum of CDs@TGM-IL.

The fluorescence performance of CDs@TGM-IL and the effect of inhibiting protein aggregation. (a) Excitation–emission matrix fluorescence spectra of CDs. (b) Excitation–emission matrix fluorescence spectra of CDs@TGM-IL. (c) Emission spectra of CDs excited at different excitation wavelengths. (d)Two-dimensional excitation–emission spectra of CDs. (e) CD spectra of Aβ42 in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL at different concentrations. (f) ThT kinetic aggregation profiles to quantify the inhibition of Aβ42 in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL at different concentrations. (g) CD spectra of lysozyme in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL at different concentrations. (h) ThT kinetic aggregation profiles to quantify the inhibition of lysozyme in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL at different concentrations.

CDs@TGM-IL disaggregated mature protein fibrils. (a-c) AFM image of Aβ42 fibrils co-incubated with 0, 1 and 25 μg/mL CDs@TGM-IL for 24 h. (d) CD spectra of Aβ42 fibrils in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL. (e) ThT kinetic aggregation profiles to quantify the disaggregation of Aβ42 fibrils in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL. (f) CD spectra of lysozyme fibrils in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL. (g) ThT kinetic aggregation profiles to quantify the disaggregation of lysozyme fibrils in the presence of CDs@TGM-IL or CDs. Tyrosine inner fluorescence assay of Aβ42 monomeric samples (20 μM) that were cultured with different concentrations of CDs (h) and CDs@TGM-IL (i).

CDs@TGM-IL Scavenged ROS. (a) Kinetic curves of DPPH reduction. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of DPPH after 10 min reaction with various concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL. (c) The CDs@TGM-IL concentra tion-dependent elimination efficiencies of DPPH. (d) The absorption spectrums of NBT after 10 min of the reaction with O2 −. (e) Plot of the rate of elimination of O2 − by CDs@TGM-IL as a function of concentration. (f) The absorption spectrums of MB the reaction with OH at different concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL. (g) The CDs@TGM-IL concentration-dependent elimination efficiencies of OH. (h) Uv-vis absorption spectra at different concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL reacting with 1O2. (i) 1O2 scavenging efficiency at different concentrations of TGM-IL@CDs.

The biocompatibility of CDs@TGM-IL. (a) Cytotoxicity of CDs and CDs@TGM-IL on PC12 cells. (b) CDs@TGM-IL alleviate Aβ42-induced cytotoxicity. (c) H&E staining of the main organs (Heart, Liver, Spleen, Lung, Kidney) after PBS and CDs@TGM-IL injection.

Effects of CDs@TGM-IL on AD mice. (a) Fluorescence signals observed in the brain tissues of mice 24 h after tail vein injection of CDs@TGM-IL (b) Corresponding fluorescence signal value of CDs@TGM-IL (c) H&E staining and (d) Nissl staining. (e) Quantitative analysis of Nissl bodies. (f) Immunohistochemical analysis of Aβ42 deposition. (g) Results for quantification of the proportion of amyloid plaques. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001).
Schematic Representation for the Synthesis of the CDs@TGM-IL.

head.ce:title: Functionalized carbon dots with guanidine salt ionic liquid regulate oxidative damage and amyloid aggregation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Air Force Medical University, Xi'an 710032, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Air Force Medical University, Xi'’an 710032, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An imbalance in the brain microenvironment, involving oxidative stress and β-amyloid (Aβ) accumulation, is thought to be one of the primary characteristics of early Alzheimer's disease (AD). To address the intricate pathophysiology of AD, therapeutic approaches that can concurrently control several diseases in the AD microenvironment are desperately needed. This study created a guanidine salt ionic liquid functionalized carbon dots (CDs@TGM-IL) to mitigate Aβ aggregation-induced cytotoxicity and scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) simultaneously. In vitro studies have shown that CDs@TGM-IL can effectively inhibit Aβ42 protein aggregation, disaggregate mature Aβ42 fibrils, and effectively remove ROS. In vivo studies have found that CDs@TGM-IL can cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and improve cognitive performance in AD mice. Just as importantly, CDs@TGM-IL has been shown to have unparalleled biocompatibility. This means that CDs@TGM-IL is expected to be a possible treatment for AD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Approximately 10 % of adults 65 years of age or older have AD, a persistent neurological ailment that has a sluggish onset and gradually gets worse [1,2]. Research has revealed that the brain starts to gradually alter over ten years prior to the illness developing [3]. Anomalous extracellular Aβ plaque formation is the primary sign of AD [4,5]. After being repeatedly cleaved by β- and γ-secretase from amyloid precursor proteins, Aβ peptides easily self-assemble into extremely stable β-lamel-rich aggregates, which in turn create neurotoxic Aβ oligomers and aggregates [6–8]. These Aβ aggregates eventually cause neurotoxicity because they keep building up in the brain's extracellular matrix. Furthermore, the creation of amyloid plaques and amyloid misfolding and aggregation are intimately linked to oxidative stress in the brain [9,10]. On the one hand, Aβ oligomers cause extracellular ROS generation and excessive calcium influx into neurons by disrupting the activation of N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptors. This finally results in mitochondrial malfunction. On the other hand, extracellular Aβ oligomers build up to form Aβ fibrils and senile plaques, which cause oxidative stress and apoptosis [11]. The control of APP modification and related secretase activity is significantly influenced by oxidative stress. Oxidative stress results in the production of oxidants and oxidative products, which can elevate the expression of APP and worsen Aβ aggregation [12]. Thus, the development of an integrated nanotherapeutic platform that can scavenge reactive oxygen species and inhibit and disaggregate Aβ42 fibrils could represent an important step in the treatment of AD.

Based on the above assumptions, various Aβ42 inhibitors, including small molecules [13–17], peptides [18–20], carbon quantum dots [21–25], and nanoparticles [26–31], have been investigated for the treatment of AD. As a result of their superior biocompatibility, photophysical stability, synthesis flexibility, and multifunction, CDs have garnered the most interest in biological applications [32–34]. Qin-Ying Li et al. [35] produced three Cr-derived CDs by reacting Congo red (CR) with three distinct reducing carbon reaction sources and ammonium persulfate. The resulting CDs can either suppress protein aggregation or scavenge free radicals, or it can activate red fluorescence imaging of protein aggregates. Lina Bao et al. [36] created chiral CDs using ternary carbon sources, chiral carbon sources like L/D-cysteine, carbon sources like citric acid, and chiral inducers like β-cyclodextrin. Among them, D-Cys-CDs have the capacity to eliminate intracellular reactive oxygen species, have a strong anti-inflammatory impact, and lessen the cytotoxicity of Aβ42-Cu2+. You Jung Chung et al. [37] reported on red light response CDs targeting Aβ and their therapeutic function as photodynamic nanomodulators to inhibit toxic Aβ aggregation in vitro and in vivo spatiotemporal. The use of CDs in AD treatment has been thoroughly studied by members of our study team. Chaoren Yan et al. [38] designed a multifunctional nanocomponent (yCDs-Ce6) by co-assembly of a photosensitizer (Ce6) and a yellow fluorescent CDs to endow yCDs-Ce6 with photodynamic and photothermal therapy (PDT and PTT) functions. Self-assembly of glycosylated carbon dots (gCD) and epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) was used to prepare a high performance nanomaterial (gCDs-E). gCDs-E can not only inhibit the fibrillation and disaggregate Aβ fibrils, but also effectively inhibit the activity of Candida albicans. gCDs-E has the ability to penetrate the BBB [39]. Xu Shao et al. [40] used a solvfree carbonization process to prepare novel photoresponsive carbon nanodots (CNDs), CNDs with large conjugated domains can interact with proteins through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, showing good BBB permeability. Xiuyun Cao et al. [41] used the solvothermal method to synthesize tannic acid (TA)-derived carbon dots (TACD), which inhibited amyloid aggregation with an efficiency as high as 98.16 % and disaggregated mature protein fibrils with an efficiency as high as 97.97 %.

A lot of ionic liquids researches are shifting toward the biological science and medicine because of their highly adjustable nature and exceptional performance [42–45]. In certain amino acids, like creatinine and arginine, the guanidine group serves as a crucial functional group that is crucial to biomedicine [46–49]. Over the past few decades, guanidine salts have been employed extensively in investigations on protein folding [50–53]. Pillai et al. [54] cocultured lysozyme proteins with ethyl ammonium nitrate (EAN) and tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquids, respectively, and atomic force microscopy showed that the addition of EAN and (TMGA) ILs resulted in thicker and thinner amyloid fibrils, respectively. Moreover, it has been demonstrated that in non-neuronal cell lines, the antidiabetic medication metformin inhibits the death of cells due to oxidative stress. [55]. Luoyao Yang et al. [56] demonstrated that metformin could improve PB-induced mitochondrial fragmentation and promote energy supply and cell survival through the antioxidant effect generated by AMPK/Nrf2 pathway activation. Zamanian et al. [57] reviewed the neuroprotective effects of agmatine in PD, focusing on oxidative stress, inflammation, and molecular mechanisms.

Most of the previous studies have only targeted a single target, but the pathogenesis of AD is very complex, so the research effect is difficult to meet expectations. In this work, we present a novel approach to inhibit AD with previously unheard-of efficiency, taking into account these two potential pathogenic causes. A new nanomaterial (CDs@TGM-IL) was created by assembling CDs with tetramethylguanidinyl acetate, and its potential to cure AD was examined (Scheme 1 ). Compared to CDs previously examined, CDs functionalized with ionic liquids exhibit greater biocompatibility and protein affinity. CDs@TGM-IL can effectively reduce Aβ-mediated cytotoxicity by inhibiting Aβ aggregation and misfolding, and CDs@TGM-IL can effectively remove ROS and reduce mitochondrial damage caused by oxidative stress. In addition, CDs@TGM-IL can cross the BBB and be metabolized by organs such as the liver and kidney. At the same time, the study confirms that CDs@TGM-IL alleviates the cognitive impairment of AD mice.

Using aluminum chloride hexahydrate as a catalyst and O-phenylene diamine and catechol as carbon sources, solvfree carbonation was used to create the CDs. The CDs was then modified with tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid to create CDs@TGM-IL. TEM was used to characterize the morphology and particle size of the CDs@TGM-IL and CDs. The mean particle diameters of CDs and CDs@TGM-IL are 2.7 nm and 3.5 nm, respectively, and they are uniformly distributed without any indications of aggregation (Fig. 1a, b). The height of CDs@TGM-IL is approximately 4.0 nm, while the height of the CDs is between 2.0 and 3.5 nm (Fig. 1c, d).

CDs@TGM-IL was analyzed for elemental content and structure using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Three characteristic peaks for C1s, N1s, and O1s can be seen in the entire XPS spectrum at 285, 400, and 531 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e). The C1s band is split into three binding energy peaks at 283.4, 285.2, and 287.7 eV, which correspond to C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1f). Two peaks in the O1s spectrum can be seen at 529.4 eV and 530.2 eV, that is, which represent the CO and C-O/O-H groups (Fig. 1g). Two peaks in the N1s spectrum can be seen in Fig. 1h, and they correspond to NH and NC, respectively, at 395.6 and 397.8 eV. The surface/boundary functional groups of CDs@TGM-IL were examined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The stretching vibrations of CO (1132 cm−1), CN (1301–1365 cm−1), CC (1487 and 1507 cm−1), CO (1648 cm−1), O-H/N-H (3380 and 3250 cm−1) and aromatic CH bending (879 cm−1 and 677 cm−1) are all apparent as Fig. 1i. CDs@TGM-IL is primarily made up of a polyaromatic structure with nitrogen and oxygen functional groups, according to the vibrational absorption of the bonds. The stretching vibration peak of the amide bond (C=O) in CDs@TGM-IL was seen at 1648 cm−1, in contrast to CDs, because of the amide reaction between the amino group of the CDs and the carboxylic acid group of the tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid. Using Raman spectroscopy, the molecular structure of CDs@TGM-IL was investigated. The Raman spectra of CDs@TGM-IL exhibits the characteristic D and G bands, as depicted in Fig. 1j. These bands are situated at about 1393 cm−1 and 1564 cm−1, respectively. The fact that the two peaks' intensity ratio is roughly 0.96 (ID/IG), which indicates that CDs@TGM-IL is made up of both disordered and graphene-like structures. The CDs ID/IG is about 0.91(Fig. S1), indicating that CDs@TGM-IL has more C-atom crystal defects than CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of the excitation-emission matrix were measured for CDs@TGM-IL and CDs with an excitation range of 200 to 900 nm and an emission range of 200 to 900 nm in order to better investigate the fluorescence features of these materials. A universal fluorescence excitation peak (400–600 nm) and a fluorescence emission peak (550–700 nm) are present in CDs. The fluorescence spectrum of the excitation-emission matrix shows that CDs have good fluorescence properties(Fig. 2a). As can be seen in Fig. 2c and d, the ideal wavelengths for CDs emission and excitation are 530 nm and 600 nm, respectively. The fluorescence properties of CDs@TGM-IL are similar to those of CDs(Fig. 2b). This also indicates that tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid on CDs surface does not change the original fluorescence performance of CDs, CDs@TGM-IL excellent fluorescence performance is also expected to be used in the field of biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of Aβ42 and lysozyme protein aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we also examined the potential of CDs@TGM-IL to efficiently block Aβ42 and lysozyme aggregation. The inhibitory effect of varying doses of CDs@TGM-IL on Aβ42 and lysozyme protein aggregation was investigated using circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy over a 24 h period. The CD spectrum, as depicted in Fig. 2e, showed that after 24 h of culture in the control group, the negative peak at 208 nm was weakened and the negative value at 218 nm was enhanced. This suggested that during the growth of Aβ42 protein, the β-fold structure increased and the A-helix structure decreased, and that Aβ42 protein misgrew and folded, which tended to form Aβ42 amyloid aggregates. Erroneous development of Aβ42 was broken when it was co-cultured with CDs@TGM-IL for a duration of 24 h. The CD spectrum showed that the positive peak at 195 nm became the negative peak and the negative peak at 218 nm became the positive peak upon the addition of CDs@TGM-IL to the Aβ42 protein culture medium. The trend became increasingly evident as the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL increased while Aβ42 was co-cultured. According to this,CDs@TGM-IL successfully suppresses the rise in β-fold structure, which stops Aβ42 from growing incorrectly. CDs@TGM-IL prevents lysozyme protein misaggregation with the same regularity, as demonstrated by an analysis of Fig. 2g. Different concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL were co-cultured with Aβ42 for 7 days in order to investigate if CDs@TGM-IL might suppress Aβ42 misaggregation for an extended period of time. Fig. 2f demonstrates the long-lasting suppression of Aβ42 misaggregation by CDs@TGM-IL, with an up to 90 % inhibition rate seen at 50 μg/mL concentration. Different concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL and CDs were co-cultured with lysozyme protein for 7 days in order to examine the role of tetramethylguanidinyl acetate on the surface of CDs@TGM-IL in inhibiting the misfolding and aggregation of amyloid proteins. The inhibitory effects of different concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL and CDs on lysozyme protein aggregation were obtained. In Fig. 2h, this is displayed. Following 7 days of co-cultivation, the 50 μg/mL CDs@TGM-IL showed a 92 % inhibition rate, compared to just 56 % for the same concentration of CDs. In summary,CDs@TGM-IL inhibits lysozyme protein misfolding more effectively than CDs. The tetramethylguanidinium acetate ionic liquid on the surface might be responsible for the increased inhibitory action. The guanidinium group has a higher affinity for amyloid due to its abundance of hydrogen atoms and polynucleotide affinity. It can connect to amyloid by strong hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic contact, which inhibits amyloid misfolding. AFM was used to analyze the morphology of lysozyme amyloid aggregates following a 24 h culture period. After 24 h of culture, the lysozyme control group misfolded to create long, thick fibrils, as seen in Fig. S2. The fragmentation and fragmentation of lysozyme amyloid aggregates started to happen when the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL added exceeded 25 μg/mL. After a 24 h incubation period, the thickness of the lysozyme aggregates was measured (Fig. S3). The statistical plot demonstrates that after incubating lysozyme protein alone for 24 h, the thickness of lysozyme aggregates can reach >45 nm. The lysozyme aggregates were much thinner after CDs@TGM-IL were introduced to the lysozyme protein culture media. The lysozyme aggregates were approximately 9 nm thick when 100 μg/mL of CDs@TGM-IL was introduced. This implies that CDs@TGM-IL have a concentration-dependent inhibitory effect on the misgrowth folding of lysozyme proteins. CDs@TGM-IL has a higher inhibitory protein aggregation efficiency at lower concentrations than some other works published in recent years (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disaggregation of mature Aβ42 fibrils and lysozyme fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: The next step involved investigating the disaggregation of CDs@TGM-IL on mature Aβ42 and lysozyme fibrils. The disaggregation of Aβ42 fibrils grown with varying doses of CDs@TGM-IL for a 24 h period was examined using AFM (Fig. 3a-c). With a rise in CDs@TGM-IL concentration, Aβ42 fibrils got smaller and thinner. The TEM pictures acquired following a 24 h incubation period with varying doses of CDs@TGM-IL and lysozyme fibrils support a similar result (Fig. S4). As demonstrated in Fig. 3d, following a 24 h culture, the control group's positive peak at 195 nm and negative peak at 218 nm remained comparatively robust, suggesting that the mature Aβ42 fibrils were unable to disaggregate. The β-fold structure was reduced when the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL cocultured with Aβ42 fibrils rose, as evidenced by the gradual transformation of the positive value at 195 nm to a negative value and the gradual conversion of the negative peak at 218 nm to a positive peak. As demonstrated by the study of Fig. 3f, CDs@TGM-IL disaggregated lysozyme protein fibrils with the same regularity. Different concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL were co-cultured with Aβ42 fibrils for 7 days in order to investigate if CDs@TGM-IL could disaggregate Aβ42 fibrils for an extended period of time. With a disaggregation of up to 95 % at a dose of 50 μg/mL, Fig. 3e illustrated the lengthy disaggregation of mature Aβ42 fibrils by CDs@TGM-IL. After that, various concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL and CDs were co-cultured with mature lysozyme fibrils for seven days in order to further investigate the function of tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid on the surface of CDs@TGM-IL in disaggregating mature amyloid fibrils(Fig. 3g). After seven days of cultivation, the control group's fluorescence intensity was roughly 64. Upon adding 10 μg/mL CDs, the fluorescence intensity decreased to around 57. However, the fluorescence intensity was around 26 when 10 μg/mL CDs@TGM-IL were introduced. This implies that CDs@TGM-IL can disaggregate lysozyme fibrils more effectively than CDs at lower doses. The disaggregation rate of 50 μg/mL CDs@TGM-IL with lysozyme fibrils reached around 90 % after 7 days of incubation, whereas only 54 % of CDs at the same concentration disaggregated. With a stronger hydrogen bond and hydrophobic interaction with the lysozyme fibrils, the guanidinium group on the surface of CDs@TGM-IL can more effectively break the lysozyme fibrils. Tyrosine fluorescence displayed a concentration-dependent drop in peak intensity at 307 nm when Aβ42 was co-incubated with CDs and CDs@TGM-IL, as illustrated in Fig. 3h and i. CDs@TGM-IL were also more effective at quenching tyrosine fluorescence than CDs. The results show that the tyrosine fluorescence can be quenched by the hydrophobic region and aromatic ring structure in CDs. The tetramethylguanidine group on the surface of CDs@TGM-IL strengthens the hydrophobic contact with Aβ42, which results in a stronger tyrosine fluorescence quenching effect. We investigated the structural remodeling of Aβ42 pentamer induced by tetramethylguanidine using molecular dynamics simulations (Fig. S5a). In the 100 ns simulation, the Radius of gyration (Rg) value of the system ranged from 1.41 to 1.58 nm, and at 100 ns, the Rg value was 1.47 nm (Fig. S5b). The Root Mean Square Deviation (RMSD) value fluctuated slightly in the first 20 ns of simulation, but with the extension of simulation time, the RMSD value fluctuated less and gradually became stable (Fig. S5c). According to the RMSD and Rg data, the system quickly reached the equilibrium state in 100 ns simulation, and the system tends to be stable and convergent. Root mean square fluctuation (RMSF) reflects the flexibility of amino acid sites. It can be seen that the head and tail residues fluctuated significantly more than the middle residues (Fig. S5d). In the 100 ns molecular dynamics simulation, Aβ42 pentamer and tetramethylguanidine group can form hydrogen bond interaction (Fig. S5e). As shown in Fig. S5f, the content of Aβ42 pentamer β-sheet was significantly reduced. The last frame of the dynamic trajectory was visually analyzed. As shown in Fig. S5g, the Asn27 residue of protein E chain formed hydrogen bonding with nitrogen atoms of small molecules, and other hydrophobic interactions were represented by the eyelash shape on the two-dimensional interaction diagram. The molecular dynamics simulation results show that tetramethylguanidine can bind with Aβ42 through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interaction, and effectively reduce the β-sheet content of Aβ42 pentamer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The scavenging performance of ROS by CDs@TGM-IL
ce:section.ce:para: The scavenging effects of various concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL on the DPPH radical, superoxide anion (O2 −), hydroxyl radical (OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) were evaluated in order to investigate the scavenging capabilities of CDs@TGM-IL for ROS. As demonstrated in Fig. 4a, the control group's absorption intensity of the UV–Vis light spectrum stayed essentially constant over the course of the 600 s test, suggesting that DPPH radicals may exist steadily during the test period. As the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL increased, the absorption peak at 517 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectra steadily dropped (Fig. 4b). The DPPH radical scavenging rate increased as the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL increased, and it reached 73 % when the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL was 100 μg/mL (Fig. 4c). This could be as a result of the unpaired electrons in DPPH being neutralized and the scavenging effect of DPPH radicals being achieved when CDs@TGM-IL is co-cultured with DPPH due to the CDs' potent hydrogen supply ability. O2 − is one of the most prevalent ROS in vivo and is crucial to the oxidative stress theory. This decrease in UV absorption value at 560 nm became more pronounced as CDs@TGM-IL concentration increased (Fig. 4d). This implies that CDs@TGM-IL's ability to scavenge O2 − is concentration-dependent. The remarkable capacity of CDs@TGM-IL to eliminate O2 − is demonstrated by the clearance rate of O2 − reaching 70 % at a concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 4e). This might be because the nitrogen atom in the guanidine group on the surface of CDs@TGM-IL has the capacity to be protonated, resulting in the formation of a positively charged group in solution [58]. This positively charged group has a higher propensity to bind to and quench O2 −. The most harmful ROS in the body, according to studies, is OH. The decrease in UV absorbance value at 660 nm was more pronounced as the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL was added, as seen in Fig. 4f. This implies a concentration-dependent scavenging action of CDs@TGM-IL on OH. CDs@TGM-IL has an excellent ability to remove OH, as evidenced by the 50 % of the OH removed when the concentration was 100 μg/mL (Fig. 4g). CDs@TGM-IL may be able to remove *OH due to the reduced phenolic hydroxyl and amino groups on the CDs surface and the easily protonated guanidine group contained in the tetramethylguanidine acetate ionic liquid. Phenolic hydroxyl groups, amino groups and protonated guanidinium groups are able to provide hydrogen ions and thus react with *OH. The absorption peak at 410 nm vanished in control 2 as compared to control 1, as seen in Fig. 4h. This suggested that the MB created 1O2 upon illumination, which suppressed the DPBF absorption peak. The UV–Vis absorption peak of DPBF was restored and 1O2 was eliminated by CDs@TGM-IL. The solvent effect may be the cause of the peak's shift to the right in Fig. 4h following the addition of CDs@TGM-IL. The clearance efficiency was as high as 89 % when the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL reached 100 μg/mL (Fig. 4i). As shown in Fig. S6, CDs had the ability to scavengers DPPH radicals, O2 −, OH, 1O2, while tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid can scavengers O2 −, OH and DPPH radicals. Since 1O2 is a highly electrophilic reactive oxygen species and the guanidine group in tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid is extremely easily protonated, the tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid may not be able to remove 1O2. Tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid and CDs are less efficient at scavenging ROS than CDs@TGM-IL. In conclusion, the excellent ROS scavenging ability of CDs@TGM-IL was attributed to tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid and CDs.

We evaluated the cytotoxicity of CDs@TGM-IL using PC12 cells. A specific cell line known as PC12 cells was created from a pheochromocytoma found in the rat adrenal medulla. It displayed characteristics common to neuroendocrine cells. Its beneficial qualities have led to its extensive usage in studies on neurophysiology and neuropharmacology. As demonstrated in Fig. 5a, the cell survival rate approached 100 % at concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL <50 μg/mL. Upon reaching 200 μg/mL of CDs@TGM-IL, almost 90 % of the cells were still surviving. This suggests that CDs@TGM-IL is essentially free of biotoxicity. On the other hand, the cell survival rate dropped dramatically at concentrations of CDs >50 μg/mL, and only around 58 % of cells survived at concentrations of 100 μg/mL of CDs. The biocompatibility of CDs is much enhanced by tetramethylguanidinyl acetate ionic liquid, it may be concluded. In addition, we conducted cytotoxicity tests with Aβ42 aggregates and discovered that the cytotoxicity of Aβ42 aggregates progressively reduced as the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL increased (Fig. 5b). This suggests that CDs@TGM-IL can effectively alleviate Aβ-mediated cytotoxicity. In combination with the above studies, the optimal concentration of CDs@TGM-IL in vitro is 50 μg/mL, and the minimum concentration should not be lower than 25 μg/mL. This has important implications for in vivo studies. We further studied their histocompatibility. As shown in Fig. 5c, we obtained mouse organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, after intravenously injecting CDs@TGM-IL solution for 14 consecutive days. After staining with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), it was found that the tissue sections of the samples treated with CDs@TGM-IL did not show obvious tissue inflammation, damage and other abnormal changes compared with the control group. The results show that the nanodrug assembly system CDs@TGM-IL has good biocompatibility and can be used for in vivo studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cross the BBB and removal of amyloid plaques in the brain
ce:section.ce:para: The BBB is the first obstacle to AD therapy as the tightly-joined and adherently-joined endothelial cells of the BBB block the entry of most inhibitors. In vitro tissue imaging (Fig. 6a) showed that after CDs@TGM-IL treatment at different times, obvious fluorescence signals could be seen in mouse brain tissues, and the fluorescence intensity after 0.5–6h of CDs@TGM-IL injection was more significant than other periods (Fig. 6b). This indicates that CDs@TGM-IL can cross the blood-brain barrier and gradually excrete from the brain after 6 h. As shown in Fig. S7a-f, drug was mainly metabolized in the liver and kidney, and partly metabolized in the spleen. The metabolic time in the liver was about 6 h, and the metabolic time in the kidney and spleen was longer, and the metabolic time was >12 h. As can be seen from Fig. S7g, there was a strong fluorescence signal value in the brain, which indicated that CDs@TGM-IL can not only cross the BBB, but also stayed in the brain for a longer time at a larger concentration. This may be related to the strong hydrophobicity of CDs@TGM-IL and the easily protonated guanidine on its surface, which enhanced the binding between CDs@TGM-IL and Aβ proteins.

To evaluate the therapeutic effect of CDs@TGM-IL for AD, 7 month-old APP/PS1 transgenic AD mice were randomly assigned and injected with formulations through the tail vein every two days for totally ten times. After that, nesting test was executed to evaluate the ability of CDs@TGM-IL to improve behaviors in AD mice (Fig. S8). The results showed that CDs@TGM-IL can effectively improve cognitive function in AD mice. Neurons were beneficial in learning and memory. According to Nissl staining (Fig. 6d and e), CDs@TGM-IL reversed the less ening in the number of Nissl bodies and the phenomenon of more vacuolation in AD mice, thus restoring neuronal cells to that of healthy mice, which indicated the neuroprotective effect of CDs@TGM-IL. H&E staining of brain showed CDs@TGM-IL could repair brain cell morphology (Fig. 6c), which was similar as Nissl staining. The in vivo therapeutic effect of CDs@TGM-IL on AD was further evaluated at the biological level. In addition, it can also be seen from Fig. 6c and d that CDs@TGM-IL has good biocompatibility in the brain. The immunohistochemical staining result suggested that CDs@TGM-IL could effectively remove toxic protein in the brain by reducing Aβ42 deposition (Fig. 6f and g). The above results indicate that CDs@TGM-IL treatment can significantly mitigate pathological changes in the brain tissue of mice with cognitive impairment, and have a reparative effect on hippocampal neuronal damage.

The synthesized CDs@TGM-IL displayed an exceptional capacity to inhibit amyloid aggregation and disaggregation of mature amyloid in vitro, exhibiting an efficiency of over 90 %. Additionally, the tyrosine fluorescence test confirmed the inherent affinity and targeting of CDs@TGM-IL to Aβ42. Furthermore, CDs@TGM-IL shown an effective capacity to scavenge ROS in vitro, with scavenging efficiency for DPPH, O2 −, OH and 1O2 being 73 %, 70 %, 50 % and 89 % at 100 μg/mL CDs@TGM-IL, respectively. CDs@TGM-IL also exhibits outstanding biocompatibility. The cell survival rate is about 100 % when the concentration of CDs@TGM-IL is <50 μg/mL, and it can remain above 90 % even at high concentrations of CDs@TGM-IL. Moreover, CDs@TGM-IL also reduced the cytotoxicity of Aβ42 aggregates. In vivo studies have demonstrated that CDs@TGM-IL improves cognitive function in AD mice by inhibit inhibiting Aβ42 monomer aggregation and promoting the breakdown and removal of Aβ42 fibrils. CDs@TGM-IL is expected to provide encouraging new perspectives on the possible applications of nanotechnology and guanidine salt ionic liquids in the treatment of AD.

Chao Wang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tongtong Hou: Resources, Investigation. Xu Shao: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Chaoli Wang: Resources, Methodology. Xin Wang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Ping Guan: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Yaoguo Wu: Supervision, Project administration. Xiaoling Hu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi (2022GY-198 and 2023-YBGY-295).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.141531.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controlling amyloid fibril properties via ionic liquids: the representative case of ethylammonium nitrate and tetramethylguanidinium acetate on the amyloidogenesis of lysozyme
The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters
